segments.text: Welcome to the World Wide Center of Math. We've been taking a look today at some
trig functions. Today, now we're going to take a look at some trig identities.
We don't really have much time or space to prove all of these identities, so if
you're looking for a strong proof of all of them, you might as well go
ahead online or do some research to see the proofs of these identities, or
you can go ahead and check out our Precalculus book. Either one works. So
the Pythagorean identities are the first set of identities we're going to look at.
It's the only set that we're going to prove, so let's take a look at a
triangle XY and hypotenuse R that would correspond to this XY
coordinate. And we know that X squared plus Y squared equals R squared
because it's a right triangle, so we're going to divide the whole thing
through by R squared and rearrange it a little bit. So we have X over R
quantity all squared plus Y over R quantity all squared equals 1, but we
know from taking a look at trig already that this is just cosine of theta and
sine of theta. So we have cosine squared theta plus sine squared theta
equals 1. That's probably the most important trig identity. It's something
you're going to constantly see, and you're going to need to be
able to reduce equations that have cosine squared theta plus sine squared
theta. You might need to rearrange equations that have values like those
in order to reduce it to this, but this is one of the main trig identities
we're going to encounter. If you go ahead for cosine squared theta plus
sine squared theta equals 1, if you go ahead and divide the whole entire
thing by cosine squared theta, it gives you the second identity 1 plus
tangent squared theta equals secant squared theta, or you could go through
and divide by sine squared theta. You get cotangent squared theta plus
1 equals cosecant squared theta. So now we're going to take a
look at the sum and difference identities. The plus and minus
signs are not there to confuse you. You just need to make
sure that you're looking at the correct convention. So if we
have a cosine of theta plus phi, the expanded version is going to be
cosine of theta cosine of phi minus. So you have that minus and
plus on the top and bottom, excuse me, both on the top correspond.
So it's going to be cosine theta cosine phi minus sine theta sine
phi. Other way around, it would be minus and a plus. And then sine
plus or minus theta and phi. The signs stay the same. So if you have
plus, you have plus in the middle here. If you have minus, you have
minus. And it's a sine theta cosine phi plus or minus cosine theta sine
phi. That identity is normally really useful in go ahead and in
proving these other identities. We could go ahead and separate this.
So it could be sine of two theta equals sine of theta plus sine of
theta, excuse me, sine of theta plus theta, all one sine. And then
you simply have sine of theta plus theta. And what it would be is sine
theta cosine theta plus cosine theta sine theta, just two cosine
theta sine theta. And then cosine two theta, you could go through a
similar process using the cosine sum identity. Half angle
identities. Again, you could do so by setting this angle as two
theta and this angle is just theta. And that's how you might
come up with these identities. We tend to try to stay away from
them a little bit because the plus or minus can really play a
factor in changing our equations. And then we have co-function
identities. There's only three of them written out here. There's
actually three more. So sine of pi over two minus theta is
cosine of theta. You could also do cosine of pi over two minus
theta is sine theta. Tangent pi over two minus theta is cotangent
of theta. Conversely, cotangent of theta, excuse me,
cotangent of pi over two minus theta is tan theta. And then
secant pi over two minus theta cosecant of theta cosecant of
pi over two minus theta would give you secant of theta. And
then last you have the reduction identities. These are
actually pretty easy to visualize. So if you have a sine
of negative theta, it's going to give you negative sine of
theta. So if you're going ahead and think of an angle
in say the first quadrant, that's going to be positive
here. And when you take the negative angle, what it's going
to do is send that angle below the x axis. So that
makes sense that sine of negative theta is negative sine
of what theta is. If you think of cosine of negative theta
also in the first quadrant, you have a positive value of cosine
and then you go ahead and take negative of that angle. Well,
it's just going to correspond to the angle on the opposite
side of the positive x axis or in quadrant four. And it's
just going to still be positive because cosine is
positive in one and four. And then tangent of theta is a
very similar case with sine of theta. So we're going to use
these identities in verifying some trig equations. It's
going to be really important to be able to use all of these
identities to manipulate trig equations so they're a little
bit easier to work with or they look a little bit more
like what we want them to look like. So verifying
identities, there's a couple things that you can use.
Obviously, you want to use algebra to manipulate the
equation. Obviously, you want to use all the identities
that are possible. You want to make sure that you're
taking the two sides of the equations equal. Look for
factorizations. Again, that's something that's really
important in the Pythagorean identities. A lot of
factorizations are going to be used in that. Do not add
or multiply both sides. So basically what that means is
we could not take this equation up here and to
verify this identity that this side equals this side,
we can't subtract cosine squared theta from both sides
or we can't multiply the whole equation through. So
don't add or multiply on both sides. When in doubt,
simply stay on one side. And then obviously, you want
to combine like terms and separate other terms. That's
very intuitive. So we're going to jump right into this
example. Sine to the fourth theta plus cosine squared
theta equals cosine to the fourth theta plus sine
squared theta. So we're going to go ahead and work with
the left side of this equation and lead the right side
completely alone and see if we can manipulate this to
become that. So the first thing I'm going to do is
make this a sine squared squared. But we know that
sine squared theta from our first trig identity, we

sine squared is one minus cosine squared. So now

we have verified that those are equal. So now

equals one. Now we're going to go ahead and change

theta over cosine squared theta. And this is going
to be one over cosine squared theta. So as you

That's pretty sweet because we can combine what's on

What I'm going to do to manipulate the left

So cosine of theta minus sine of theta times

And this is cosine of theta plus sine of theta

As we know from one of our double angle

that cosine squared of theta minus sine

this without manipulating the right side at

a look at, I'm going to erase some of this
and rewrite it, is sine two theta times

two. So what I'm going to do here is first
replace the sine of two theta with the

if you're being intuitive here, you're

squared terms. And again we're going to
see that Pythagorean identity and this
value is just going to be two. And
that's what we had it equal to
originally. So again we have verified
another identity. And again just
remember the most important steps for
verifying identities when you're
given a problem like this. Look for
all possible uses of your trig
identities. Make sure that you don't add
or multiply terms on both sides of the
equation. And make sure you're
combining like terms, factoring,
getting denominators to be the same,
numerators to be the same,
at all possible junctions. That way
you have the cleanest and best
looking sides to work with and
manipulate. So that is how you verify
trig identities.
